result:
The last time I rode the overnight train south, the carriage windows were thick with condensed breath and the smell of burnt coffee from the samovar at the end of the corridor. I had not planned the trip; I simply walked to the station after an argument with someone who mattered and bought the first ticket that would take me more than five hundred kilometres away. The clerk printed the boarding pass as if she were passing me a prescription: “Two berths, upper and lower, track nine, the slow one.” I nodded, relieved that the world still contained slow things.  

In my compartment was an old woman eating sunflower seeds from the pouch of her apron, a boy of maybe twelve holding a violin case to his chest like a shield, and—once the train lurched free of the city—an attendant who showed us how to collapse the seats into beds. The woman spoke first, unprompted, perhaps to distract us from the creaking metal that sounded too much like a wounded animal: “Every time this line leaves the capital, someone gives birth before we cross the river. True story from ’83.” She cracked another seed, tongue working shell from kernel with elegant speed, then swept the husks into her palm. A tiny storm in miniature.  

The boy did not take the upper bunk. He said his instrument needed to stay horizontal or its ribs would warp in the night air. I volunteered for the climb because ladders in moving trains make me feel like a child on a playground, a brief regression toward lightness. I folded myself up beneath the ceiling and watched the cabin shrink to a diorama: the grandmotherly silhouette, the boy as monolith, the violin case shrinking into an icon on his lap.  

Hours passed in railroad time, elastic, unclockable. I woke to find the boy standing in the corridor, sliding door open, playing silent scales with his left hand. No bow, only fingers on invisible strings. I climbed down and asked if he wanted tea. He said yes in the solemn way children do when offered adult rituals. In the vestibule between carriages the floor jittered sideways, a live thing. We balanced paper cups that quivered under the fluorescent tube. Steam rose and fogged my glasses while he told me he was on his way to an audition at the conservatory. Not the capital’s conservatory, but an older, smaller one farther south where teaching was strict and practice rooms smelled of mothballs and pine. He had memorized partitas, caprices, a Shostakovich concerto; his teacher had called him once-in-a-generation. He worried his fingers were still too small for tenths.  

I told him fingers grow even if childhood doesn’t. The sentence surprised me; I hadn’t known I believed it. We returned to the compartment where the woman was now snoring, seed husks still cupped like rosary beads in her upturned palm. The boy laid the violin across her empty seat as carefully as if it were an infant, tucked a scarf under its scroll like a pillow, then folded himself into the top bunk I had vacated. I took the lower one. He whispered from above: “Do you think trains remember the people who ride them?” I said maybe the tracks remember better, because iron outlives wood. He made a small sound of approval and was soon asleep.  

Somewhere past the forest fire glow we reached a viaduct, the kind that appears in postcards as pure silhouette. I opened the blind just enough to see the moon laid flat on water running black between canyon walls. The reflection looked like aability recumbent: an image submitting gratefully to its own undoing. I thought of all the letters I had never sent, how paper curls under stamps and travels slower than regret. On the bunk above, the boy’s breathing synced with the rails, four-four time.  

At dawn the attendant knocked softly and offered menus whose plastic laminate had cracked into maps of tiny nations. I bought the grandmotherly woman black bread and piroshki; she accepted them like communion, tearing off bits with arthritic economy. She pressed two foil-wrapped candies into my hand. “For the road,” she said, so I would understand this was not charity but continuity.  

When the train finally sighed into the southern platform, the air smelled of cedar and diesel. The boy clutched his violin and searched the crowd for the figure he most wanted or feared. I never learned whether a teacher or parent stepped forward; I left before the scene resolved, unwilling to witness either triumph or disappointment, those opposite faces of the same coin.  

I walked along the quay where salt wind combed my hair into knots. The city was smaller than I remembered from childhood holidays, yet larger because now I had agency. Cafés opened their wooden blinds like eyelids; gulls quarrelled over fish heads; an accordionist played a tango so slowly it fractured into separable heartbeats. I bought coffee in a thick glass and sat on the harbour wall warming my palms against the curve, realizing the argument I had fled was itself only freight rolling down rails laid long before either of us spoke. Distance had already begun to loosen its knotted syllables.  

By evening I found a guesthouse whose proprietor kept dogs that had mastered the art of sighing humanly when denied scraps from the kitchen table. She asked no questions except whether I would like dinner at eight. I wanted to tell her everything: the sunflower-eating oracle, the boy with the cracked-parent violin, the train that remembered us like ballast. Instead I said yes please and watched the dogs circle once more before collapsing into rug-bordered sleep, proof that peace is achievable without explanation.  

That night I dreamed I was back on the train but it now ran in a loop around the room where I had argued. Each time we passed the window I saw myself and the other person speaking, but the roar of wheels ate the words before they reached the glass. On the sixth circuit the silhouettes stopped mid-sentence and turned to wave. The sight was unbearably ordinary, like waving to a neighbourur. I woke in a sweat, small hours dripping into guttered candles.  

The next morning I walked through the old bazaar and bought an old telegram blank from a paper dealer; it was already stamped SOUTH EXPRESS—CANCELLED, a relic from the era when urgency travelled by hammered brass key. On the reverse I wrote one line: “Distance is only accumulation of still points.” I did not address it to anyone. Instead I slipped it between the strings of a street musician’s battered guitar case as he took break to drink from a communal fountain. Maybe the paper will travel again in a chord progression, maybe in a busker’s pocket to the next town, maybe nowhere. Either way, the motion is enough.  

Years later I still don’t know whether the violin boy passed his audition, or if the train birth superstition holds true in the third millennium. But whenever I hear the metal-on-metal lullaby of departure, I feel the floor tilt through the soles of memory, carrying me not farther from that quarrel but somehow within kissing distance of its original wound, close enough to see the scar has become a map legend, a small star indicating where tenderness was invented. And if the iron tracks remember, then archivists, we travellers merely scribble marginalia on passing landscapes until ink becomes weather.
